title: Poulet Rôti
subtitle: French roast chicken — butter, herbs, and a high oven
description: The French approach to roast chicken is direct and uncluttered: a good bird, generously buttered inside and out, seasoned well, placed breast-side down for the first half of the cook (to baste the breast meat from the inside as the juices run down), then flipped and finished at high heat for crisp, golden skin. The pan juices are deglazed with a splash of white wine and water to make a simple jus — nothing else needed.
sourceNotes: Poulet rôti is a foundational recipe of French home cooking. The breast-side-down technique appears in Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) and is standard in classical French roasting method. Jacques Pépin's La Technique (1976) details the same approach.

recipeTools.notes: Just large enough to hold the bird snugly — too large and the juices spread thin and burn.
Target 75 °C at the thickest part of the thigh, away from the bone.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin, unthickened sauce made by deglazing a roasting tin with liquid (wine, stock, or water) and scraping up the browned fond. Lighter than gravy, which is thickened with flour.
Leaving cooked meat off the heat before carving. Juices redistribute back through the muscle fibres rather than running straight out onto the board.

ingredients.notes: About 1.8 kg — a free-range bird if possible
Crushed, to stuff the cavity — unpeeled is fine
Squeezed inside the cavity, then left in
Be generous — chicken needs more salt than you think

steps: Remove the chicken from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Heat the oven to 220 °C fan (240 °C / gas 9).

Pat the chicken dry inside and out. Rub the softened butter all over the bird and under the skin of the breast if possible. Season generously all over with salt and pepper. Stuff the cavity with the thyme, garlic, and the squeezed lemon half.
Place the chicken breast-side down in the roasting tin. Roast at 220 °C fan for 20 minutes.
Flip the bird breast-side up. Reduce the oven to 190 °C fan (210 °C / gas 7). Roast for a further 45–55 minutes, basting once halfway through with the pan juices.
The chicken is done when the juices run clear when the thigh is pierced with a skewer, and a thermometer reads 75 °C at the thickest part of the thigh away from the bone.
Transfer the chicken to a warm board and tent loosely with foil. Rest for 15 minutes.

Make the jus: place the roasting tin over a medium hob heat. Pour in the white wine and let it bubble, scraping up all the browned fond. Add the water and simmer for 2–3 minutes. Strain through a sieve if you want a clear jus. Taste and season.
Carve the chicken and serve with the jus alongside.

content.text: Starting the chicken breast-side down is the French trick: the juices from the back and thighs drip downward through the breast meat as the bird roasts, keeping it moist. Flipping for the final stretch gives the breast skin time to crisp. Bring the bird to room temperature first — a cold chicken goes into the oven unevenly.

Heat the oven to 220 °C fan. Pat {{whole-chicken}} of chicken dry inside and out. Rub {{butter-unsalted}} of softened butter all over — and under the breast skin if you can lift it without tearing. Season generously with salt and pepper. Stuff the cavity with the thyme, garlic, and squeezed lemon half. Place breast-side down in the roasting tin and roast for 20 minutes at 220 °C fan.
Flip the bird breast-side up, reduce the oven to 190 °C fan, and roast for 45–55 minutes more, basting once. The chicken is done at 75 °C in the thigh, or when the juices run clear.
Transfer to a board and tent with foil. Rest for 15 minutes — the
period is what makes carving produce slices rather than a flood of juice onto the board.

Place the roasting tin over a medium hob heat. Pour in {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and {{water}} of water. Scrape up all the fond, let it bubble for 2–3 minutes, and strain into a warm jug. This is the
. Carve the chicken and serve with the jus alongside.
